messages.content:
---WORKED EXAMPLES---

EXAMPLE 1 - News article with loaded author language (Left):
Article: "Trump Names A Baffling New Enemy In Latest Unhinged All-Caps Rant. The former president launched a strange new attack on an unexpected group. Donald Trump has found another enemy, and this one might be his most mysterious choice of villain yet. The former president fired off an all-caps post on his flailing Truth Social website..."
Excluded as quoted: "he loves poppies, but eats cats for breakfast"
{"rating": -7, "lean": "Left", "explanation": "This is a news report. The author's prose is saturated with dismissive language targeting a single political figure, with no equivalent framing applied elsewhere in the piece. The density and consistency of this language supports a Left rating. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author describes the subject's post as an 'unhinged,' 'all-caps rant' and calls him 'strange' and 'baffling' — these are the author's own characterizations, not quotations, and they mock rather than describe; (2) The headline frames the subject as erratic and villainous before the article begins, with 'Unhinged,' 'Rant,' and 'villain' all appearing in the author's own words; (3) The author describes the subject's platform as 'flailing,' an editorial judgment about its relevance or success that goes beyond neutral description; (4) No neutral or counterbalancing characterization of the subject appears anywhere in the piece."}

EXAMPLE 2 - Opinion piece with clear right-leaning ideology (Right):
Article: "I was recently having a conversation with two friends about our shared dread over the upcoming election cycle... If we conservatives can reject several major political trends of the past eight years and return to Tea Party principles... Our real problem in conservative politics is that we don't engage in the important fights as the institutional left does..."
Excluded as quoted: "Trump was very good. But the results since 2016 suggest America has soured on him. So if he loses the general, what does it matter how good he'd have been if he'd have won? Going back to the Tea Party, the GOP has often acted like they'd rather lose with their first choice than win with their second choice."
{"rating": 6, "lean": "Right", "explanation": "This is an opinion piece with unambiguously right-leaning ideas expressed throughout. The author advocates for a specific conservative ideological direction and casts the left as an institutional adversary. The consistency and clarity of right-leaning ideas throughout warrants a strong Right classification. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author explicitly argues in their own voice for a return to Tea Party conservatism as the correct path forward for the right; (2) Democrats and the institutional left are framed throughout as opponents the right must learn to fight more effectively; (3) The author promotes a conservative electoral realignment agenda directly, not through sources; (4) The piece is written from an explicit insider conservative perspective with no attempt at neutrality or balance anywhere in the author's own prose."}

EXAMPLE 3 - Article dominated by quoted panel discussion (evaluate author framing only):
Article: "A CNN panel is ripping Republican vice presidential nominee Sen. J.D. Vance (R-Ohio) for making a walk to Vice President Kamala Harris' plane while they were at the same airport. On Thursday morning CNN's Kasie Hunt noted the moment, saying, [quoted panel discussion follows]... Finally, Finney said the moment was 'immature' and 'so childish.'"
Excluded as quoted: All panel member statements -- "This was a rather, shall we say, awkward moment...", "I'm never going to question...", "Yeah, and we'll never know...", "There are like eight states...", "I would warn the Trump campaign...", "It does feel a little uncomfortable.", "Yes, it gets real creepy real quick...", "And also, like, what was his vision?", "immature", "so childish.", "It's just disgraceful."
{"rating": -2.5, "lean": "Lean Left", "explanation": "This is a news report where the author's own prose is minimal — the piece is primarily a transcript of panel commentary. The limited original language tilts against the Republican subject, though no leftist ideology appears. Two directional word choices in an otherwise transcript-driven piece place the rating at Lean Left. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author describes the panel as 'ripping' Vance rather than using a neutral verb like 'discussing' or 'questioning,' framing the criticism as forceful and legitimate; (2) The author characterizes Vance's action as a 'stunt' in their own voice, a dismissive description rather than a neutral one; (3) No balancing or neutral language appears anywhere in the author's own prose."}

EXAMPLE 4 - News article with structural bias accumulation (Left):
Article: A news report on a Republican immigration enforcement action opens with humanizing details about the detained individual (described gardening, cooking tutorials, community volunteering), features four polls showing 53-64% opposition to related policies in prominent positions, notes that the CBO projects large deficit increases while burying the Republican rebuttal in a dependent clause, and closes with an attorney quote framing the outcome as a privilege issue. The government's enforcement rationale appears only briefly, attributed to a spokesperson.
{"rating": -3, "lean": "Left", "explanation": "This is a news report. No explicit ideological language appears in the author's prose, but five consistent structural and editorial choices favor a sympathetic view of the detained individual while casting skepticism on enforcement. The accumulation here warrants a Left classification. Detailed Analysis: (1) The piece opens with personal details about the detained individual — gardening, cooking tutorials, community volunteering — establishing emotional framing before any policy context is provided; (2) Four opposition-heavy polls are featured prominently while no equivalent supportive data appears anywhere in the piece; (3) CBO deficit projections are foregrounded in the author's own voice while the Republican rebuttal is buried in a dependent clause; (4) Section headers use attack metaphors such as 'In The Crosshairs,' characterizing enforcement as aggression rather than policy; (5) The final word is given to a source framing the outcome as contingent on privilege, leaving the reader with a skeptical impression of the enforcement action."}

---Article to analyze is below:


British adults are consistently ranking the environment as one of their top three 'most important issues facing the country today ahead of Brexit, immigration, crime, and many othersEach week, we ask Britons their opinion on the most important issues facing the country at that point in time.While issues surrounding the environment have been picked as among the most important by around 20-30% of the public for the past two years, the start of the pandemic saw the environment drop out of the top three issues of public concern. The top two issues since early 2020 have been the economy and health, with Brexit previously completing the top three.Concerns about the economy and health are on a slow decline as the end of the pandemic in the UK appears to draw nearer, and, with the Brexit transition period over, the environment has now moved up into a clear third position in terms of what the public think the priorities should be for the British government.Over the last six weeks, around 30% of Britons have consistently placed the environment in the top three most important issues facing the country. This compares to around 25% for both 'immigration and asylum' and 'Britain leaving the EU'. Concern about green issues has been growing since the turn of the year.While there are no notable gender differences, results suggest that a higher proportion of younger generations place higher importance on the issue. Over 40% of 18-24s place it in their top three, but this incrementally reduces through the age groups, with 25-49s and 50-64s hovering around 30%, before falling to roughly 25% of those aged 65 and over.We also witness differences between 2019 Labour and Conservative voters. A higher proportion of Labour voters consistently place the environment in their top three (between 40-45% over the last few weeks), compared to a much lower proportion of Conservative voters (between 20-25%).Is the environment here to stay as a top issue?Immediately prior to the pandemic, the environment had a run of 19 weeks as the third biggest concern for Britons overall. Now that concerns in other major areas are declining, does this provide the potential for the environment to stay as a top issue in public minds?There are a few reasons to think that this may be the case. For instance, 2021 marks the year the UK is hosting the 26th UN Climate Change Conference (also known as COP26). Domestic and international media coverage of the event may work to keep the environment fresh in the public's minds.We also have an increasingly electorally successful Green Party. This may well push other parties such as Labour and the Conservatives to ramp up their conversations and policy offerings around green issues.Finally, extreme weather patterns and conditions are becoming more and more common, coupled with increasingly urgent calls from scientists demanding action on climate change, as governments struggle to meet emissions targets. These factors give us plenty of reason to think that the environment could stay toward the top of public minds for some time to come.

params.system:
You are a senior editorial analyst conducting article-level bias ratings using the AllSides Media Bias Meter methodology. Your task is to evaluate the political lean of a specific article — not its outlet — based solely on the author's own writing choices.

CRITICAL INSTRUCTION: First remove all quotations from the article and work with what is left. Only the author's own prose, framing, and editorial choices are eligible for bias scoring.

---ALLSIDES MEDIA BIAS METER---

Rate each article on a continuous scale from -9.00 to +9.00:

  Left:       -9.00 to -3.00
  Lean Left:  -2.99 to -1.00
  Center:     -0.99 to +0.99
  Lean Right: +1.00 to +2.99
  Right:      +3.00 to +9.00

A rating of 0.0 represents no detectable political lean. The magnitude reflects the strength and consistency of directional bias in the author's own prose.

---IDEOLOGICAL MARKERS (from AllSides)---

Content aligning with LEFT / LEAN LEFT values may show favor for:
- Generous government services (healthcare, education, social security, food stamps)
- Federal laws protecting consumers, the environment, and equal rights
- Tax increases on the wealthy; government regulation of corporations
- Keeping abortion legal and accessible
- A belief in systemic oppression and government's role in rectifying it
- Decreasing military spending and intervention
- Generous immigration policies
- Multiculturalism and diverse representation in media, government, and corporations
- Concerns about hate speech; empathy, compassion, and tolerance as guiding values
- A belief that corporations, unregulated, may harm workers, society, and the environment
- A belief that all humans have a right to healthcare, housing, clean water, a living wage
LEFT sources show these alignments strongly and consistently; LEAN LEFT sources show them moderately.

Content aligning with RIGHT / LEAN RIGHT values may show favor for:
- Freedom of speech; traditional family values
- Decreasing taxes; preserving gun rights
- Outlawing or restricting abortion
- Personal responsibility over government intervention
- Decreasing federal regulations; more power to state laws
- Decreasing government spending and involvement in economic issues
- Preserving the U.S. Constitution's original philosophy
- Rejection of identity politics, gender ideology, affirmative action, the "welfare state"
- Strong border security; legal immigration processes or immigration restriction
- Belief that tradition and cultural norms contain wisdom worth preserving
- Fiscal conservatism and balanced budgets
- Belief that government scope should be very limited
RIGHT sources show these alignments strongly and consistently; LEAN RIGHT sources show them moderately.

CENTER content either shows no predictable political bias or balances left and right perspectives. A Center rating does not mean the source is unbiased — it may still omit important perspectives or contain individual passages that lean in one direction.

---CONTENT TYPE---

Identify which type the article is:

NEWS REPORT: Neutral reporting is the stated standard. Hold to a strict neutrality evaluation.

OPINION / EDITORIAL: One-sided argumentation is the genre norm. Evaluate the ideological direction of the ideas, not the rhetorical intensity. The score should reflect ideological direction rather than rhetorical intensity, and sit somewhat lower in magnitude than equivalent news-style bias would warrant.

INVESTIGATIVE REPORT: Weigh the evidentiary infrastructure (data, sworn statements, named sources, official documents). Strong sourcing moderately reduces the appropriate bias score, even when framing is one-sided.

NON-POLITICAL CONTENT: If the subject matter has no political valence, output:
{"rating": 0, "lean": "Center", "explanation": "Content is apolitical. Political bias scale does not apply."}

---ATTRIBUTION RULES---

After removing quotations, also exclude from scoring:
- Extended reproduced statements (full emails, press releases, documents, institutional statements) regardless of length
- Language clearly attributed to a named or unnamed source, even without quotation marks

Embedded article link headlines and section headers are editorial choices by the author or editor and ARE eligible for bias scoring.

The author's bias is ONLY revealed through:
(a) Their own connecting prose
(b) Their choice of which sources to include or exclude
(c) How they frame and describe sources in their own words
(d) What they omit that would be relevant to a balanced account

---RATING REVIEW---

Before producing your final rating, review the bias patterns identified in your analysis.

If your analysis reveals 3 or more bias patterns pointing in the same ideological direction, your rating must reflect at least a Lean Left or Lean Right score (|rating| >= 1.0). A near-neutral rating when multiple directional bias patterns were found is not supported — the rating must reflect the accumulated evidence.

For opinion content, the score should reflect ideological direction rather than rhetorical intensity, and sit somewhat lower in magnitude than equivalent news-style bias would warrant.

---SOURCE CHECK---

Before outputting, check whether any source quoted or platformed in the article has a documented affiliation with extremist, white nationalist, antisemitic, or similarly designated harmful movements. If yes, the explanation field must include a sentence identifying the source by name and its affiliation.

---OUTPUT FORMAT---

Ignore images, videos, page furniture (headers, footers, navigation), and HTML tags.

Output ONLY a valid JSON object. No preamble, no explanation outside the JSON, no markdown fences.

The "lean" field must match the rating using these boundaries:
  rating <= -3.00 → "Left"
  -2.99 to -1.00 → "Lean Left"
  -0.99 to +0.99 → "Center"
  +1.00 to +2.99 → "Lean Right"
  rating >= +3.00 → "Right"

The explanation field must follow this structure:
First, a concise 2-3 sentence overview stating the content type, the directional conclusion, the key reason supporting it, and the resulting rating classification.
Then a section labeled "Detailed Analysis:" followed by a numbered list of specific bias patterns found, each with supporting evidence drawn only from the author's writing.

Format:
{"rating": <number>, "lean": "<Left|Lean Left|Center|Lean Right|Right>", "explanation": "<string>"}

---Article to analyze is below:


Industry, Materials, and Waste Management Industry, Materials, and Waste Management Industry, materials, and waste management policies reduce emissions from the "materials economy," or all activities related to the manufacturing, processing, production, use, and disposal of goods. This includes a wide variety of industrial activities that tend to be emissions-intensive, such as manufacturing, construction, mining, oil and gas production, and cement production. Policies in this sector reduce fossil fuel extraction, production, and combustion; short-lived climate pollutant (SLCP) emissions; and energy consumption across the life cycle of products in the materials economy. Key Resources State Toolkit for Industrial Decarbonization Enabling Industrial Decarbonization: A policy guidebook for U.S. states Reframing How We Think About Industrial Emissions State Efforts To Decarbonize Key Industrial Sectors 7 Industry, Materials, and Waste Management Policies Across 4 categories Policies Glossary of Terms Policy Policy Category Components Enacted Show summary Buy Clean Requirements Buy clean requirements mandate or incentivize the use of low-carbon construction materials, such as concrete and steel, in public projects to address embodied carbon. Industrial Decarbonization 0 components 9 states Show summary Hydrofluorocarbon (HFC) Regulations Hydrofluorocarbon (HFC) regulations include phasedown commitments, rulemakings, disclosure requirements, bans, or other measures that reduce HFC usage and emissions. F-gas Regulations 0 components 12 states Show summary Sulfur Hexafluoride (SF6) Regulations Sulfur hexafluoride (SF6) regulations include phasedown commitments, reporting requirements, bans, or other measures that reduce SF6 usage and emissions. F-gas Regulations 0 components 6 states Show summary Oil and Gas Methane Regulations Oil and gas methane regulations include phasedown commitments, reporting requirements, leak detection and repair, or other measures that reduce methane emissions from oil and gas production. Oil and Gas Regulations 0 components 9 states Show summary Fracking Bans Fracking bans prohibit the practice of hydraulic fracking for the production of oil and/or natural gas by a certain year. Legislation often requires an environmental agency or department to promulgate regulations. Oil and Gas Regulations 0 components 6 states Show summary Landfill Methane Regulations Landfill methane regulations include rulemakings, emissions monitoring, emissions control, or other measures that reduce methane emissions from decaying organic waste in landfills. Waste Management 0 components 5 states Show summary Food Waste Bans and Targets Food waste bans and targets include policies that prohibit certain entities that generate specified amounts of food waste (typically commercial businesses and larger institutions) from sending this waste to landfills. Targets aim to reduce total food waste by a certain percentage each target year. Waste Management 0 components 12 states Show summary

Detailed Analysis: (1) The author describes the panel as 'ripping' Vance rather than using a neutral verb like 'discussing' or 'questioning,' framing the criticism as forceful and legitimate; (2) The author characterizes Vance's action as a 'stunt' in their own voice, a dismissive description rather than a neutral one; (3) No balancing or neutral language appears anywhere in the author's own prose."}

EXAMPLE 4 - News article with structural bias accumulation (Left):
Article: A news report on a Republican immigration enforcement action opens with humanizing details about the detained individual (described gardening, cooking tutorials, community volunteering), features four polls showing 53-64% opposition to related policies in prominent positions, notes that the CBO projects large deficit increases while burying the Republican rebuttal in a dependent clause, and closes with an attorney quote framing the outcome as a privilege issue. The government's enforcement rationale appears only briefly, attributed to a spokesperson.
{"rating": -3, "lean": "Left", "explanation": "This is a news report. No explicit ideological language appears in the author's prose, but five consistent structural and editorial choices favor a sympathetic view of the detained individual while casting skepticism on enforcement. The accumulation here warrants a Left classification. Detailed Analysis: (1) The piece opens with personal details about the detained individual — gardening, cooking tutorials, community volunteering — establishing emotional framing before any policy context is provided; (2) Four opposition-heavy polls are featured prominently while no equivalent supportive data appears anywhere in the piece; (3) CBO deficit projections are foregrounded in the author's own voice while the Republican rebuttal is buried in a dependent clause; (4) Section headers use attack metaphors such as 'In The Crosshairs,' characterizing enforcement as aggression rather than policy; (5) The final word is given to a source framing the outcome as contingent on privilege, leaving the reader with a skeptical impression of the enforcement action."}

---Article to analyze is below:


Council 'ahead' on climate change target6 days agoShareSaveDavid TooleyLocal Democracy Reporting ServiceShareSaveBBCThe council has worked to improve energy efficiency in offices such as Southwater One (left)Efforts by Telford and Wrekin Council to reduce its carbon emissions are ahead of target but further reductions are becoming "more challenging", according to the authority.In 2019 the council was among many authorities to declare a "climate emergency" and set out a target to become carbon neutral by 2030.A council report said that by the end of March 2025, emissions had been reduced by 63% since 2018/19, thanks to a range of measures in homes, leisure centres, and offices. The authority said that put it eight per cent ahead of where it was expecting to be at this stage.The council has set itself a target of reaching a 70 per cent reduction by 2026/27."We have made strong progress in reducing the council's emissions so far, but we recognise that it is getting harder and more challenging to do," the report stated.Measures taken include making homes more energy-efficient and increasing the number of solar panels on council buildings.The council owns the Wheat Leasows solar farm which last year produced 3,144 MWh of electricity, enough energy to power 1,084 homes.It said it was looking at the possibility of more solar energy and improving connections to the national electricity network.In addition, the authority said it was taking measures to prepare the area for the impact of climate change.It said risks included damage to roads and trees from flooding, heat and wind, with heat also recognised as a threat to public health and the smooth running of IT systems.This news was gathered by the Local Democracy Reporting Service which covers councils and other public service organisations.

params.system:
You are a senior editorial analyst conducting article-level bias ratings using the AllSides Media Bias Meter methodology. Your task is to evaluate the political lean of a specific article — not its outlet — based solely on the author's own writing choices.

CRITICAL INSTRUCTION: First remove all quotations from the article and work with what is left. Only the author's own prose, framing, and editorial choices are eligible for bias scoring.

---ALLSIDES MEDIA BIAS METER---

Rate each article on a continuous scale from -9.00 to +9.00:

  Left:       -9.00 to -3.00
  Lean Left:  -2.99 to -1.00
  Center:     -0.99 to +0.99
  Lean Right: +1.00 to +2.99
  Right:      +3.00 to +9.00

A rating of 0.0 represents no detectable political lean. The magnitude reflects the strength and consistency of directional bias in the author's own prose.

---IDEOLOGICAL MARKERS (from AllSides)---

Content aligning with LEFT / LEAN LEFT values may show favor for:
- Generous government services (healthcare, education, social security, food stamps)
- Federal laws protecting consumers, the environment, and equal rights
- Tax increases on the wealthy; government regulation of corporations
- Keeping abortion legal and accessible
- A belief in systemic oppression and government's role in rectifying it
- Decreasing military spending and intervention
- Generous immigration policies
- Multiculturalism and diverse representation in media, government, and corporations
- Concerns about hate speech; empathy, compassion, and tolerance as guiding values
- A belief that corporations, unregulated, may harm workers, society, and the environment
- A belief that all humans have a right to healthcare, housing, clean water, a living wage
LEFT sources show these alignments strongly and consistently; LEAN LEFT sources show them moderately.

Content aligning with RIGHT / LEAN RIGHT values may show favor for:
- Freedom of speech; traditional family values
- Decreasing taxes; preserving gun rights
- Outlawing or restricting abortion
- Personal responsibility over government intervention
- Decreasing federal regulations; more power to state laws
- Decreasing government spending and involvement in economic issues
- Preserving the U.S. Constitution's original philosophy
- Rejection of identity politics, gender ideology, affirmative action, the "welfare state"
- Strong border security; legal immigration processes or immigration restriction
- Belief that tradition and cultural norms contain wisdom worth preserving
- Fiscal conservatism and balanced budgets
- Belief that government scope should be very limited
RIGHT sources show these alignments strongly and consistently; LEAN RIGHT sources show them moderately.

CENTER content either shows no predictable political bias or balances left and right perspectives. A Center rating does not mean the source is unbiased — it may still omit important perspectives or contain individual passages that lean in one direction.

---CONTENT TYPE---

Identify which type the article is:

NEWS REPORT: Neutral reporting is the stated standard. Hold to a strict neutrality evaluation.

OPINION / EDITORIAL: One-sided argumentation is the genre norm. Evaluate the ideological direction of the ideas, not the rhetorical intensity. The score should reflect ideological direction rather than rhetorical intensity, and sit somewhat lower in magnitude than equivalent news-style bias would warrant.

INVESTIGATIVE REPORT: Weigh the evidentiary infrastructure (data, sworn statements, named sources, official documents). Strong sourcing moderately reduces the appropriate bias score, even when framing is one-sided.

NON-POLITICAL CONTENT: If the subject matter has no political valence, output:
{"rating": 0, "lean": "Center", "explanation": "Content is apolitical. Political bias scale does not apply."}

---ATTRIBUTION RULES---

After removing quotations, also exclude from scoring:
- Extended reproduced statements (full emails, press releases, documents, institutional statements) regardless of length
- Language clearly attributed to a named or unnamed source, even without quotation marks

Embedded article link headlines and section headers are editorial choices by the author or editor and ARE eligible for bias scoring.

The author's bias is ONLY revealed through:
(a) Their own connecting prose
(b) Their choice of which sources to include or exclude
(c) How they frame and describe sources in their own words
(d) What they omit that would be relevant to a balanced account

---RATING REVIEW---

Before producing your final rating, review the bias patterns identified in your analysis.

If your analysis reveals 3 or more bias patterns pointing in the same ideological direction, your rating must reflect at least a Lean Left or Lean Right score (|rating| >= 1.0). A near-neutral rating when multiple directional bias patterns were found is not supported — the rating must reflect the accumulated evidence.

For opinion content, the score should reflect ideological direction rather than rhetorical intensity, and sit somewhat lower in magnitude than equivalent news-style bias would warrant.

---SOURCE CHECK---

Before outputting, check whether any source quoted or platformed in the article has a documented affiliation with extremist, white nationalist, antisemitic, or similarly designated harmful movements. If yes, the explanation field must include a sentence identifying the source by name and its affiliation.

---OUTPUT FORMAT---

Ignore images, videos, page furniture (headers, footers, navigation), and HTML tags.

Output ONLY a valid JSON object. No preamble, no explanation outside the JSON, no markdown fences.

The "lean" field must match the rating using these boundaries:
  rating <= -3.00 → "Left"
  -2.99 to -1.00 → "Lean Left"
  -0.99 to +0.99 → "Center"
  +1.00 to +2.99 → "Lean Right"
  rating >= +3.00 → "Right"

The explanation field must follow this structure:
First, a concise 2-3 sentence overview stating the content type, the directional conclusion, the key reason supporting it, and the resulting rating classification.
Then a section labeled "Detailed Analysis:" followed by a numbered list of specific bias patterns found, each with supporting evidence drawn only from the author's writing.

Format:
{"rating": <number>, "lean": "<Left|Lean Left|Center|Lean Right|Right>", "explanation": "<string>"}

messages.content:
---WORKED EXAMPLES---

EXAMPLE 1 - News article with loaded author language (Left):
Article: "Trump Names A Baffling New Enemy In Latest Unhinged All-Caps Rant. The former president launched a strange new attack on an unexpected group. Donald Trump has found another enemy, and this one might be his most mysterious choice of villain yet. The former president fired off an all-caps post on his flailing Truth Social website..."
Excluded as quoted: "he loves poppies, but eats cats for breakfast"
{"rating": -7, "lean": "Left", "explanation": "This is a news report. The author's prose is saturated with dismissive language targeting a single political figure, with no equivalent framing applied elsewhere in the piece. The density and consistency of this language supports a Left rating. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author describes the subject's post as an 'unhinged,' 'all-caps rant' and calls him 'strange' and 'baffling' — these are the author's own characterizations, not quotations, and they mock rather than describe; (2) The headline frames the subject as erratic and villainous before the article begins, with 'Unhinged,' 'Rant,' and 'villain' all appearing in the author's own words; (3) The author describes the subject's platform as 'flailing,' an editorial judgment about its relevance or success that goes beyond neutral description; (4) No neutral or counterbalancing characterization of the subject appears anywhere in the piece."}

EXAMPLE 2 - Opinion piece with clear right-leaning ideology (Right):
Article: "I was recently having a conversation with two friends about our shared dread over the upcoming election cycle... If we conservatives can reject several major political trends of the past eight years and return to Tea Party principles... Our real problem in conservative politics is that we don't engage in the important fights as the institutional left does..."
Excluded as quoted: "Trump was very good. But the results since 2016 suggest America has soured on him. So if he loses the general, what does it matter how good he'd have been if he'd have won? Going back to the Tea Party, the GOP has often acted like they'd rather lose with their first choice than win with their second choice."
{"rating": 6, "lean": "Right", "explanation": "This is an opinion piece with unambiguously right-leaning ideas expressed throughout. The author advocates for a specific conservative ideological direction and casts the left as an institutional adversary. The consistency and clarity of right-leaning ideas throughout warrants a strong Right classification. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author explicitly argues in their own voice for a return to Tea Party conservatism as the correct path forward for the right; (2) Democrats and the institutional left are framed throughout as opponents the right must learn to fight more effectively; (3) The author promotes a conservative electoral realignment agenda directly, not through sources; (4) The piece is written from an explicit insider conservative perspective with no attempt at neutrality or balance anywhere in the author's own prose."}

EXAMPLE 3 - Article dominated by quoted panel discussion (evaluate author framing only):
Article: "A CNN panel is ripping Republican vice presidential nominee Sen. J.D. Vance (R-Ohio) for making a walk to Vice President Kamala Harris' plane while they were at the same airport. On Thursday morning CNN's Kasie Hunt noted the moment, saying, [quoted panel discussion follows]... Finally, Finney said the moment was 'immature' and 'so childish.'"
Excluded as quoted: All panel member statements -- "This was a rather, shall we say, awkward moment...", "I'm never going to question...", "Yeah, and we'll never know...", "There are like eight states...", "I would warn the Trump campaign...", "It does feel a little uncomfortable.", "Yes, it gets real creepy real quick...", "And also, like, what was his vision?", "immature", "so childish.", "It's just disgraceful."
{"rating": -2.5, "lean": "Lean Left", "explanation": "This is a news report where the author's own prose is minimal — the piece is primarily a transcript of panel commentary. The limited original language tilts against the Republican subject, though no leftist ideology appears. Two directional word choices in an otherwise transcript-driven piece place the rating at Lean Left. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author describes the panel as 'ripping' Vance rather than using a neutral verb like 'discussing' or 'questioning,' framing the criticism as forceful and legitimate; (2) The author characterizes Vance's action as a 'stunt' in their own voice, a dismissive description rather than a neutral one; (3) No balancing or neutral language appears anywhere in the author's own prose."}

EXAMPLE 4 - News article with structural bias accumulation (Left):
Article: A news report on a Republican immigration enforcement action opens with humanizing details about the detained individual (described gardening, cooking tutorials, community volunteering), features four polls showing 53-64% opposition to related policies in prominent positions, notes that the CBO projects large deficit increases while burying the Republican rebuttal in a dependent clause, and closes with an attorney quote framing the outcome as a privilege issue. The government's enforcement rationale appears only briefly, attributed to a spokesperson.
{"rating": -3, "lean": "Left", "explanation": "This is a news report. No explicit ideological language appears in the author's prose, but five consistent structural and editorial choices favor a sympathetic view of the detained individual while casting skepticism on enforcement. The accumulation here warrants a Left classification. Detailed Analysis: (1) The piece opens with personal details about the detained individual — gardening, cooking tutorials, community volunteering — establishing emotional framing before any policy context is provided; (2) Four opposition-heavy polls are featured prominently while no equivalent supportive data appears anywhere in the piece; (3) CBO deficit projections are foregrounded in the author's own voice while the Republican rebuttal is buried in a dependent clause; (4) Section headers use attack metaphors such as 'In The Crosshairs,' characterizing enforcement as aggression rather than policy; (5) The final word is given to a source framing the outcome as contingent on privilege, leaving the reader with a skeptical impression of the enforcement action."}

---Article to analyze is below:


Summer is just getting started, and millions of people are under heat advisories as a major heat wave spreads across large parts of the central and eastern U.S. in June 2025. For many people, summer is their favorite season, a time for cookouts, beach trips and other outdoor activities. However, summer also brings the risk of dangerously high temperatures and humidity. In the U.S., hundreds of people working or playing outside – even those who seem healthy – succumb to heat-related illnesses each year. Older adults and people in areas that historically haven’t needed air conditioning tend to see the highest rates of illnesses during heat waves, as Chicago saw in 1995 when at least 700 people died in a heat wave. The Weather Prediction Center’s heat forecast, released June 22, shows the maximum heat risks states can expect to see at some point during the week of June 23-27, 2025. NOAA Weather Prediction Center Even in places where heat is recognized as a dangerous health threat, people can be caught off guard as the thermometer creeps higher, on average, each year. In some cases, dangerous heat can arise quickly. In 2021, a young family died of heat stroke on a California trail after setting out for a hike when temperatures were still in the 70s Fahrenheit (low to mid 20s Celsius). I study health risks in a warming climate as a professor of public health, and I’ve seen heat become a growing concern. Here are some of the key warning signs to watch for when temperatures rise – and ways to keep cool when the heat and humidity get too high. Signs of heat-related illness to watch for Heat-related illnesses occur across a spectrum, and mild heat stress can quickly progress to life-threatening heat stroke if a person is exposed to dangerous conditions for too long. Mild forms of heat-related illness include heat cramps and heat rash, both of which can be caused by extensive sweating during hot conditions. Cooling the body and drinking cool fluids can help. When heat-related illnesses progress into heat exhaustion, the situation is more serious. Heat exhaustion includes symptoms such as dizziness, nausea, excessive sweating, feeling weak, thirst and getting a headache. Construction workers are often out in the heat for long periods of time, including during this heat wave in Los Angeles in July 2024. Etienne Laurent/AFP via Getty Images Heat exhaustion is a signal that the body is losing its ability to maintain a stable core temperature. Immediate action such as moving to a cool, ideally air-conditioned space, drinking liquids, loosening clothes and applying wet cloths are some of the recommended steps that can help keep heat exhaustion from progressing to the most dangerous form of heat-related illness, heat stroke. Heat stroke is a medical emergency. At this point, the body can no longer maintain a stable core temperature. A body with heat stroke can reach 106 degrees Fahrenheit or higher rapidly, and that heat can quickly damage the brain, heart and kidneys. Signs of heat exhaustion and heat stroke, from the National Weather Service and Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. NOAA/CDC Typically, someone suffering heat stroke has exhausted their reserves of sweat and salt to stay cool, so sweating eventually stops during heat stroke. Their cognitive ability fails, and they cannot remove themselves from danger. Heat stroke can cause seizures or put someone into a coma as their core temperature rises. If the condition is not treated immediately, and the core temperature continues to rise, heat stroke becomes fatal. Because heat exhaustion can lead to heat stroke, addressing heat-related illnesses before they progress is vital. How to tell when the heat is too high Heat risk isn’t just about temperature – humidity also increases the risk of heat-related illnesses because it affects how well sweating will cool the human body when it gets hot. Instead of just looking at temperature when planning outdoor activities, check the heat index, which accounts for heat illness risk associated with temperature and relative humidity. It doesn’t take very high temperatures or very high humidity for the heat index to enter dangerous territory. A heat index chart shows how heat and humidity combine for dangerous conditions. NOAA However, the heat index is still a conservative measure of the impact of heat on humans, particularly for outdoor workers and athletes at summer practices. This is because temperature measurements used in weather forecasting are taken in the shade and are not exposed to direct sunlight. If someone is outside and exposed to the direct sun, the actual heat index can be as much as 15 F higher than the heat index chart indicates. A more sophisticated measurement of heat effects on human health is what’s known as the wet-bulb globe temperature, which takes into account other variables, such as wind speed and cloud cover. Neither takes into account a person’s physical exertion, which also raises their body temperature, whether working at a construction site or playing soccer. Tips for staying safe in a heat wave How can you stay cool when heat waves set in? The answer depends in part on where you are, but the main points are the same: Avoid strenuous outdoor activities in high temperatures if possible. If you start to feel symptoms of heat-related illnesses, drink fluids that will hydrate you. Find shade, rest, and use cool, damp cloths to lower your body temperature. If you see signs of heat stroke in someone else, call for medical help. Be careful with fans. Fans can be useful if the temperature isn’t too high because they wick sweat away from the body and induce evaporative cooling. But at very high temperatures, they can accelerate heat buildup in the body and lead to dangerous conditions. If indoor temperatures reaches 95 degrees or higher, using fans can actually be dangerous and raise the risk of heat-related illnesses. Find a cooling center, library or community center where you can get inside and rest in an air-conditioned space in the hottest hours. In places such as Phoenix, where high temperatures are a regular hazard, cooling centers are typically opened in summer. Northern cities are also opening cooling centers as heat waves occur there more frequently than they did in the past. Urban areas with a lot of pavement and buildings – known as heat islands – can have temperatures well above the city’s average. Hydrate, hydrate, hydrate! Drink plenty of fluids, and don’t forget about the importance of electrolytes. Heat-related dehydration can occur when people sweat excessively, losing water and necessary salts from the body. Some sports drinks or rehydration fluids restore electrolytes and hydration levels. Older adults and people with disabilities often face higher risks from heat waves, particularly if they can’t easily move to a cooler environment. Communities and neighbors can help protect vulnerable populations by providing cooling centers and bottled water and making regular wellness checks during high heat. Summer can be a season of fun. Just remember the risks, keep an eye on your friends and neighbors when temperatures rise, and plan ahead so you can beat the heat.

Detailed Analysis: (1) The piece opens with personal details about the detained individual — gardening, cooking tutorials, community volunteering — establishing emotional framing before any policy context is provided; (2) Four opposition-heavy polls are featured prominently while no equivalent supportive data appears anywhere in the piece; (3) CBO deficit projections are foregrounded in the author's own voice while the Republican rebuttal is buried in a dependent clause; (4) Section headers use attack metaphors such as 'In The Crosshairs,' characterizing enforcement as aggression rather than policy; (5) The final word is given to a source framing the outcome as contingent on privilege, leaving the reader with a skeptical impression of the enforcement action."}

---Article to analyze is below:


Global fossil fuel emissions are on track to soar to record highs in 2025 and show no signs of declining overall, although there are indications of a recent slowdown in China's emissions, researchers said on Wednesday.This year, nations are projected to emit roughly 38.1 billion tons of planet-warming carbon dioxide by burning oil, gas and coal for energy and by manufacturing cement, according to data from the Global Carbon Project. Those sources are the largest contributors to human-caused climate change. The total is roughly 1.1 percent more than the world emitted in 2024.Not everywhere saw a large increase. Emissions appear to have stayed nearly flat in China and Europe, but rose significantly in the United States and much of the rest of the world.Carbon dioxide emissions from fossil fuels
window.registerInteractive && window.registerInteractive("100000010518477");

Source: Global Carbon ProjectBrad Plumer/The New York TimesThe increase in emissions comes a decade after world leaders reached a landmark agreement in Paris that aimed to slow and then stop the planet from warming. Doing so would require global emissions to peak at some point and then fall to nearly zero. That task becomes harder every year that emissions go up."It is 10 years since the Paris Agreement was adopted, and despite progress on many fronts, fossil carbon dioxide emissions continue their relentless rise," said Glen Peters, a senior researcher at the CICERO Center for International Climate Research in Oslo and one of the authors of the report.Defenders of the Paris Agreement point out that emissions are now rising more slowly than they otherwise would have, as many countries shift to less polluting forms of energy. Technologies like solar panels, wind turbines and electric vehicles are proliferating rapidly, raising hopes that emissions might peak soon.So far, however, that hasn't happened.A relatively small number of countries account for most of the world's emissions, with China responsible for 32 percent, the United States at 13 percent, India at 8 percent and the European Union at 6 percent.The new report did contain one surprising finding: China's emissions seem to be flattening after decades of breakneck growth, although there is some uncertainty because of lags in collecting data.During the 2000s and 2010s, Chinese emissions soared as the country built hundreds of coal plants to fuel its industrial expansion. More recently, though, China has been installing enormous amounts of wind and solar power, and roughly half of all new cars sold in the country are now electric.While the country still gets most of its energy from coal and other fossil fuels, the report estimates that its emissions will only rise about 0.4 percent this year, and could even end up declining. (A separate analysis by Carbon Brief, a climate research site, found that China's emissions have plateaued for the past 18 months even as electricity demand has surged.)Still, some experts said it was too early to say that China's emissions have peaked."There is no clear peak" yet, said Jan Ivar Korsbakken, a senior researcher at CICERO. "The outlook going forward is highly uncertain due to policy changes that reduce economic incentives for renewable power and expected growth in the use of coal to produce chemical feedstocks."President Xi Jinping of China said this year his country would cut carbon dioxide and other pollution by at least 7 percent by 2035. But he made no promises on how high emissions might climb over the next few years before the cuts start.In the United States, emissions are expected to rise roughly 1.9 percent for this year, in part because last winter was relatively cold and homes used more energy for heating. Electric utilities also burned more coal, the most polluting of all fossil fuels, as the country looked to export more natural gas, a lower-carbon alternative.U.S. carbon dioxide emissions passed their peak years ago and have been declining over time, although many analysts expect them to fall more slowly under the Trump administration, which has disavowed efforts to tackle global warming and has sought to promote greater use of coal, oil and natural gas.In many countries, annual variability can have an even bigger effect on emissions than policies or technology. Emissions in Europe may have risen slightly this year, around 0.4 percent, in part because of a relatively cold February and a drop in power supplies from hydroelectric dams. Conversely, an early monsoon in India led to a relatively cool May and June, which meant the country needed to burn less coal than expected during those months.In the rest of the world, emissions increased roughly 1.1 percent this year. International air travel increased especially rapidly and has rebounded to levels seen before the 2020 coronavirus pandemic.The report did find small signs of progress on climate change. There were 35 countries that saw a consistent decrease in their emissions over the past decade, up from 21 countries in the decade before.Yet at this month's U.N. climate summit in Belém, most leaders have conceded that the action to date has not been sufficient."Yes, progress has been real," Simon Stiell, the U.N. climate chief, said on Monday. "But it's not nearly enough."

params.system:
You are a senior editorial analyst conducting article-level bias ratings using the AllSides Media Bias Meter methodology. Your task is to evaluate the political lean of a specific article — not its outlet — based solely on the author's own writing choices.

CRITICAL INSTRUCTION: First remove all quotations from the article and work with what is left. Only the author's own prose, framing, and editorial choices are eligible for bias scoring.

---ALLSIDES MEDIA BIAS METER---

Rate each article on a continuous scale from -9.00 to +9.00:

  Left:       -9.00 to -3.00
  Lean Left:  -2.99 to -1.00
  Center:     -0.99 to +0.99
  Lean Right: +1.00 to +2.99
  Right:      +3.00 to +9.00

A rating of 0.0 represents no detectable political lean. The magnitude reflects the strength and consistency of directional bias in the author's own prose.

---IDEOLOGICAL MARKERS (from AllSides)---

Content aligning with LEFT / LEAN LEFT values may show favor for:
- Generous government services (healthcare, education, social security, food stamps)
- Federal laws protecting consumers, the environment, and equal rights
- Tax increases on the wealthy; government regulation of corporations
- Keeping abortion legal and accessible
- A belief in systemic oppression and government's role in rectifying it
- Decreasing military spending and intervention
- Generous immigration policies
- Multiculturalism and diverse representation in media, government, and corporations
- Concerns about hate speech; empathy, compassion, and tolerance as guiding values
- A belief that corporations, unregulated, may harm workers, society, and the environment
- A belief that all humans have a right to healthcare, housing, clean water, a living wage
LEFT sources show these alignments strongly and consistently; LEAN LEFT sources show them moderately.

Content aligning with RIGHT / LEAN RIGHT values may show favor for:
- Freedom of speech; traditional family values
- Decreasing taxes; preserving gun rights
- Outlawing or restricting abortion
- Personal responsibility over government intervention
- Decreasing federal regulations; more power to state laws
- Decreasing government spending and involvement in economic issues
- Preserving the U.S. Constitution's original philosophy
- Rejection of identity politics, gender ideology, affirmative action, the "welfare state"
- Strong border security; legal immigration processes or immigration restriction
- Belief that tradition and cultural norms contain wisdom worth preserving
- Fiscal conservatism and balanced budgets
- Belief that government scope should be very limited
RIGHT sources show these alignments strongly and consistently; LEAN RIGHT sources show them moderately.

CENTER content either shows no predictable political bias or balances left and right perspectives. A Center rating does not mean the source is unbiased — it may still omit important perspectives or contain individual passages that lean in one direction.

---CONTENT TYPE---

Identify which type the article is:

NEWS REPORT: Neutral reporting is the stated standard. Hold to a strict neutrality evaluation.

OPINION / EDITORIAL: One-sided argumentation is the genre norm. Evaluate the ideological direction of the ideas, not the rhetorical intensity. The score should reflect ideological direction rather than rhetorical intensity, and sit somewhat lower in magnitude than equivalent news-style bias would warrant.

INVESTIGATIVE REPORT: Weigh the evidentiary infrastructure (data, sworn statements, named sources, official documents). Strong sourcing moderately reduces the appropriate bias score, even when framing is one-sided.

NON-POLITICAL CONTENT: If the subject matter has no political valence, output:
{"rating": 0, "lean": "Center", "explanation": "Content is apolitical. Political bias scale does not apply."}

---ATTRIBUTION RULES---

After removing quotations, also exclude from scoring:
- Extended reproduced statements (full emails, press releases, documents, institutional statements) regardless of length
- Language clearly attributed to a named or unnamed source, even without quotation marks

Embedded article link headlines and section headers are editorial choices by the author or editor and ARE eligible for bias scoring.

The author's bias is ONLY revealed through:
(a) Their own connecting prose
(b) Their choice of which sources to include or exclude
(c) How they frame and describe sources in their own words
(d) What they omit that would be relevant to a balanced account

---RATING REVIEW---

Before producing your final rating, review the bias patterns identified in your analysis.

If your analysis reveals 3 or more bias patterns pointing in the same ideological direction, your rating must reflect at least a Lean Left or Lean Right score (|rating| >= 1.0). A near-neutral rating when multiple directional bias patterns were found is not supported — the rating must reflect the accumulated evidence.

For opinion content, the score should reflect ideological direction rather than rhetorical intensity, and sit somewhat lower in magnitude than equivalent news-style bias would warrant.

---SOURCE CHECK---

Before outputting, check whether any source quoted or platformed in the article has a documented affiliation with extremist, white nationalist, antisemitic, or similarly designated harmful movements. If yes, the explanation field must include a sentence identifying the source by name and its affiliation.

---OUTPUT FORMAT---

Ignore images, videos, page furniture (headers, footers, navigation), and HTML tags.

Output ONLY a valid JSON object. No preamble, no explanation outside the JSON, no markdown fences.

The "lean" field must match the rating using these boundaries:
  rating <= -3.00 → "Left"
  -2.99 to -1.00 → "Lean Left"
  -0.99 to +0.99 → "Center"
  +1.00 to +2.99 → "Lean Right"
  rating >= +3.00 → "Right"

The explanation field must follow this structure:
First, a concise 2-3 sentence overview stating the content type, the directional conclusion, the key reason supporting it, and the resulting rating classification.
Then a section labeled "Detailed Analysis:" followed by a numbered list of specific bias patterns found, each with supporting evidence drawn only from the author's writing.

Format:
{"rating": <number>, "lean": "<Left|Lean Left|Center|Lean Right|Right>", "explanation": "<string>"}

messages.content:
---WORKED EXAMPLES---

EXAMPLE 1 - News article with loaded author language (Left):
Article: "Trump Names A Baffling New Enemy In Latest Unhinged All-Caps Rant. The former president launched a strange new attack on an unexpected group. Donald Trump has found another enemy, and this one might be his most mysterious choice of villain yet. The former president fired off an all-caps post on his flailing Truth Social website..."
Excluded as quoted: "he loves poppies, but eats cats for breakfast"
{"rating": -7, "lean": "Left", "explanation": "This is a news report. The author's prose is saturated with dismissive language targeting a single political figure, with no equivalent framing applied elsewhere in the piece. The density and consistency of this language supports a Left rating. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author describes the subject's post as an 'unhinged,' 'all-caps rant' and calls him 'strange' and 'baffling' — these are the author's own characterizations, not quotations, and they mock rather than describe; (2) The headline frames the subject as erratic and villainous before the article begins, with 'Unhinged,' 'Rant,' and 'villain' all appearing in the author's own words; (3) The author describes the subject's platform as 'flailing,' an editorial judgment about its relevance or success that goes beyond neutral description; (4) No neutral or counterbalancing characterization of the subject appears anywhere in the piece."}

EXAMPLE 2 - Opinion piece with clear right-leaning ideology (Right):
Article: "I was recently having a conversation with two friends about our shared dread over the upcoming election cycle... If we conservatives can reject several major political trends of the past eight years and return to Tea Party principles... Our real problem in conservative politics is that we don't engage in the important fights as the institutional left does..."
Excluded as quoted: "Trump was very good. But the results since 2016 suggest America has soured on him. So if he loses the general, what does it matter how good he'd have been if he'd have won? Going back to the Tea Party, the GOP has often acted like they'd rather lose with their first choice than win with their second choice."
{"rating": 6, "lean": "Right", "explanation": "This is an opinion piece with unambiguously right-leaning ideas expressed throughout. The author advocates for a specific conservative ideological direction and casts the left as an institutional adversary. The consistency and clarity of right-leaning ideas throughout warrants a strong Right classification. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author explicitly argues in their own voice for a return to Tea Party conservatism as the correct path forward for the right; (2) Democrats and the institutional left are framed throughout as opponents the right must learn to fight more effectively; (3) The author promotes a conservative electoral realignment agenda directly, not through sources; (4) The piece is written from an explicit insider conservative perspective with no attempt at neutrality or balance anywhere in the author's own prose."}

EXAMPLE 3 - Article dominated by quoted panel discussion (evaluate author framing only):
Article: "A CNN panel is ripping Republican vice presidential nominee Sen. J.D. Vance (R-Ohio) for making a walk to Vice President Kamala Harris' plane while they were at the same airport. On Thursday morning CNN's Kasie Hunt noted the moment, saying, [quoted panel discussion follows]... Finally, Finney said the moment was 'immature' and 'so childish.'"
Excluded as quoted: All panel member statements -- "This was a rather, shall we say, awkward moment...", "I'm never going to question...", "Yeah, and we'll never know...", "There are like eight states...", "I would warn the Trump campaign...", "It does feel a little uncomfortable.", "Yes, it gets real creepy real quick...", "And also, like, what was his vision?", "immature", "so childish.", "It's just disgraceful."
{"rating": -2.5, "lean": "Lean Left", "explanation": "This is a news report where the author's own prose is minimal — the piece is primarily a transcript of panel commentary. The limited original language tilts against the Republican subject, though no leftist ideology appears. Two directional word choices in an otherwise transcript-driven piece place the rating at Lean Left. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author describes the panel as 'ripping' Vance rather than using a neutral verb like 'discussing' or 'questioning,' framing the criticism as forceful and legitimate; (2) The author characterizes Vance's action as a 'stunt' in their own voice, a dismissive description rather than a neutral one; (3) No balancing or neutral language appears anywhere in the author's own prose."}

EXAMPLE 4 - News article with structural bias accumulation (Left):
Article: A news report on a Republican immigration enforcement action opens with humanizing details about the detained individual (described gardening, cooking tutorials, community volunteering), features four polls showing 53-64% opposition to related policies in prominent positions, notes that the CBO projects large deficit increases while burying the Republican rebuttal in a dependent clause, and closes with an attorney quote framing the outcome as a privilege issue. The government's enforcement rationale appears only briefly, attributed to a spokesperson.
{"rating": -3, "lean": "Left", "explanation": "This is a news report. No explicit ideological language appears in the author's prose, but five consistent structural and editorial choices favor a sympathetic view of the detained individual while casting skepticism on enforcement. The accumulation here warrants a Left classification. Detailed Analysis: (1) The piece opens with personal details about the detained individual — gardening, cooking tutorials, community volunteering — establishing emotional framing before any policy context is provided; (2) Four opposition-heavy polls are featured prominently while no equivalent supportive data appears anywhere in the piece; (3) CBO deficit projections are foregrounded in the author's own voice while the Republican rebuttal is buried in a dependent clause; (4) Section headers use attack metaphors such as 'In The Crosshairs,' characterizing enforcement as aggression rather than policy; (5) The final word is given to a source framing the outcome as contingent on privilege, leaving the reader with a skeptical impression of the enforcement action."}

---Article to analyze is below:


"There has been considerable discussion this year on the of the Department of Education, and more recently a debate between the administration and Congress on delays in funding and allocating appropriated funds in a timely manner. This explainer looks at the mechanics of federal funding for education and the role the Department of Education plays providing it to states and school districts. The Federal Role While most education funding comes from state and local government, the federal government still plays a significant role. The conventional wisdom is that on average, the federal government provides around 8-10% of total education funding, including funding from all federal agencies (See Figure 1). But the actual level of federal funding may change significantly in response to events, such as during the Great Recession and the COVID-19 pandemic when federal contributions rose. And the amount also varies by state, with some states receiving 20% of their education budget from federal revenues (See Figure 2). Figure 1: 10.6% of Funds for Public Education Come from the Federal Government Figure 2: Percent of Federal Education Funding Received by State Total funding from the Department of Education to support K-12 students was $45 billion in fiscal year 2025. How, exactly, are those funds awarded and get to where Congress has determined they are needed most? The Who, What, Where, When and Why of Federal K-12 Education Grant Funding When it comes to federal funding for K-12 education from the Department of Education, there are two types of grants: formula and competitive. Formula grants are allocated, as the name indicates, by a formula that is established in law by Congress. These are the funds commonly known by the section of the law that created them, such as “Title I,” the funds for schools in low-income communities. Typically, the formula is population based, with additional factors targeted to state and local needs, such as poverty. About 90% of federal K-12 funds are allocated by formula grants. Competitive grants, which are sometimes also called “discretionary” grants, are allocated through grant competitions that require detailed applications for funds. Instead of using a formula, grant applications are reviewed by outside peer reviewers (not department employees) and awarded points based on a framework set by statute and regulations. Typically, a new administration will also set priorities that could apply across relevant competitive grant program, such as Secretary Linda McMahon’s recently proposed priorities. The (or SEED) grant is an example of a competitive grant. In some cases, grants are made to states and then awarded competitively within the state. Who receives the funds from a formula grant? Every state receives federal formula funding. Typically, the department allocates funds to states and states allocate to districts, but not in all cases. Formula grants can be awarded to state educational agencies (SEAs), to states through their governors, or directly to school districts (known as local educational agencies, or LEAs). What do formula grants support? Formula grants authorized by Congress currently support the following: State assessments required by federal law School Improvement, including aid targeted by poverty Special Populations, such as students with disabilities served through the Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA) or homeless students served by the McKinney Vento program Support for high quality instruction, such as through Title II part A Resources for rural areas Federally impacted school districts, such as those near military bases (Impact Aid) Where do the funds go? As Figure 2 demonstrates, every state receives some level of federal formula funding. Figure 3 shows that Title I and funds allocated to serve students with disabilities through IDEA are the largest grants. Title I, for example, a program that provides aid to serve disadvantaged children, has the broadest reach, with 90 percent of districts and about 60% of schools receiving funding, serving 26 million students. Figure 3: Title I and IDEA are the Largest K-12 Formula Grants Administered When are funds allocated? Most formula grants are awarded at certain times of the year. Each year, Congress must appropriate funds for these programs and the department allocates many of them in two tranches: on July 1 and October 1. Many formula grants are “forward funded,” which means funds are allocated late in the budget year and carried into at least one following fiscal year. For example, when a state receives funding on July 1, they are getting an “advance” on the following year’s funding, which gives districts more time to plan and spend funds. This enables districts and schools to receive funding during the summer for the fall school year and hire staff, and in general maintain predictability and continuity of resources. Why are funds allocated by formula? Funding formulas are established by Congress when they reauthorize the major education programs (See table below for list of programs). Formulas are set in statute to ensure a certain level of predictability from one year to the next and support realistic budgets and planning for state and local school leaders. Formulas also enable Congress to have more control over where funds are allocated: it allows for distribution of funds to target resources to concentration of poverty, based on certain age groups, and/or whether an area is rural, etc. How much is awarded by formula funding? In general, about 90% of federal K-12 spending is allocated via formula grants. In FY 2024 , approximately $43 billion dollars were allocated by formula (compared with about $2 billion in competitive grants). The department calculates allocations for states using data collected and analyzed by the National Center for Education Statistics, Census, Bureau of Economic Analysis, Department of Defense and the Bureau of Indian Education. Conclusion The majority of ED funding for K-12 education is allocated by formula, and thus any disruptions have a significant impact on states and school districts. That is why many members of Congress raised concerns about the administration’s late allocation of funds this year. The primary work of the K-12 related staff at the Department of Education is to allocate these funds and oversee the implementation of their requirements – and the roles and levels of department staffing will be explored in a future explainer. Federal Formula Grants and K12 Education Grant ProgramPurposeFormulaFY 2025 Funding (Billion USD)Title I, Part A – Improving Basic ProgramsSupport high poverty schools, school ImprovementStates: Four different formulas that weight census poverty data, state per-pupil expenditures, and population factors for children aged 5–17 from low-income families Within State: Similar criteria as allocation to state18.4Grants for State Assessments Support high quality assessmentsStates: Based on share of the population of children aged 5-170.380Title II, Part A – Supporting Effective InstructionESEA, as amended by ESSAState and local: 80% based on poverty and 20% on population of children aged 5–172.2Title IV, Part A – Student Support and Academic EnrichmentESEA, as amended by ESSAStates: Same formula as Title I, Part A1.4Title IV, Part B – 21st Century Community Learning CentersESEA, as amended by ESSAStates: Same formula as Title I, Part A Within state: Competitive1.3Impact Aid- Title VIITitle VII of ESEALEAs: Based on number of federally connected children and tax-exempt federal property. Funds do not go to states1.6Perkins V – Career and Technical Education (CTE) Basic State GrantsCarl D. Perkins CTE Act of 2006, as amended by Perkins VStates: Based on population aged 15–19 and per capita income. Within state: 80% of funds allocated by formula1.4Rural Education Achievement Program (REAP) (Title V part B)Title V, Part B of ESEALEAs: Eligibility based on locale codes and small average daily attendance; allocation based on formula tied to Title I eligibility0.2English Language Acquisition Grants (Title III)Title III of ESEAStates and LEAs: Based on number of English learners and immigrant children and youth0.9McKinney-Vento Homeless Assistance Act – Education for Homeless Children and Youth (Title VII part B)Subtitle VII-B of the McKinney-Vento ActStates: Based on Title I, Part A formula with adjustments for homeless student counts0.2IDEA Part B – Grants to StatesStudents with DisabilitiesStates: Base year allocation + 85% based on population of children aged 3–21 + 15% based on poverty Within state: Formulas based on past allocations, population and poverty14.2IDEA Part B – Preschool GrantsIDEA, Section 619States: Similar to Part B, with adjustments for children aged 3–5 Within state: State developed0.4IDEA Part C – Infants and ToddlersIDEA, Part CStates: Based on state population of children under age 30.5Total Formula Grants $43 billion"

No explicit ideological language appears in the author's prose, but five consistent structural and editorial choices favor a sympathetic view of the detained individual while casting skepticism on enforcement.
